sections.summary: Codex Standard for Dried Basil (CXS 345-2021), adopted 2021 and amended 2022, with the 2022 amendment updating non‑retail container labelling to be in accordance with the General Standard for the Labelling of Non‑Retail Containers of Foods (CXS 346‑2021).
Scope: applies to dried basil leaves (Ocimum spp.) offered for direct consumption, as food ingredients, or for repackaging, and explicitly excludes products for industrial processing.

Describes dried basil as products prepared from leaves of Ocimum spp. (Lamiaceae) processed by cleaning, drying, rubbing, milling and sifting into the styles listed in Section 2.2, and provides a table of species covered.
Defines dried basil as the product prepared from leaves of Ocimum spp. (Lamiaceae) and lists the specific Ocimum species/trade names covered by the standard in Table 1.

Specifies allowable product styles (whole/intact; crushed/rubbed/flaked; ground/powdered; or other clearly labelled styles) and states that particle size for ground/powdered styles is to be determined by contractual agreement between buyer and seller.
Lists permitted dried basil styles: whole/intact; crushed/rubbed/flaked; ground/powdered; and other distinct styles if labelled accordingly.

sections.section: 2.2.2 The particle size of ground/powdered styles is determined by contractual agreement between buyer and seller.
sections.summary: States that particle size of ground/powdered dried basil is determined by contractual agreement between buyer and seller.

Requires dried basil to conform to composition requirements in Annexes I and II and to have characteristic odour, flavour and colour (free from foreign or musty odours), with chemical and physical characteristics and allowable defects specified in Annexes I and II so as not to adversely affect product quality, keeping quality or package presentation.
States that dried basil as described must conform to the composition requirements set out in Annexes I and II of the standard.

Sets out quality factors: dried basil must have characteristic odour/flavour (no foreign or musty odours) and comply with chemical and physical requirements in Annex I and II, with allowed defects not affecting appearance, keeping quality or package presentation.

Requires dried basil to have a characteristic odour and flavour (which may vary by geo‑climatic conditions), be free from foreign or musty odours, and notes that typical colour may change with post‑harvest treatment.

States that the product must comply with the chemical characteristics in Annex I and the physical characteristics in Annex II, and that allowed defects must not impair the product's general appearance, keeping quality or package presentation.

Reiterates that the generic product must meet the requirements in Annex I (chemical) and Annex II (physical) and that permitted defects must not affect quality, keeping quality or package presentation.

Mandates compliance with Codex contaminant texts: products must meet the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193‑1995), the Code of Practice to prevent pyrrolizidine alkaloid contamination (CXC 74‑2014) and other relevant Codex texts, and must comply with Codex pesticide maximum residue limits.

Recommends that products covered by this standard be prepared and handled in accordance with relevant Codex hygiene texts (General Principles of Food Hygiene CXC 1-1969, Code of Hygienic Practice for Low‑Moisture Foods CXC 75-2015, Annex III on Spices and Culinary Herbs, Code of Practice for the Prevention and Reduction of Mycotoxins in Spices CXC 78-2017, and other relevant texts) and states the products should comply with any microbiological criteria established per CXG 21-1997.
Containers shall be filled as fully as practicable without impairing product quality and the fill must be consistent with a proper declaration of contents.

Products covered by this standard must be labelled in accordance with the General Standard for the Labelling of Pre‑packaged Foods (CXS 1-1985), with additional specific labelling provisions set out in the following subsections.

The common name must follow Section 2.1; blends of listed species may use the common name, a trade name may be used only if at least 80% of the product is the species listed for that trade name, and the product name may include trade name, varietal type and style per Table 1 and Section 2.2.

sections.section: 8.3 Country of origin and country of harvest
sections.summary: Country of origin must be declared on the label; country of harvest is optional, and region and year of harvest are optional.
Labelling of non‑retail containers should follow the General Standard for the Labelling of Non‑Retail Containers in Foods (CXS 346-2021).
Methods of analysis for checking compliance shall use the Recommended Methods of Analysis and Sampling (CXS 234-1999) relevant to this standard; a sampling plan is noted as to be developed.

Annex II contains Table 3 specifying physical quality characteristics and maximum limits for dried basil (by style) covering extraneous/foreign matter, insect counts, visible mould, mammalian and other excreta, insect damage and live insect limits.

sections.section: Table 3. Physical characteristics of dried basil
sections.summary: Table 3 sets maximum physical quality limits for dried basil by style: for Whole/Intact — extraneous matter 0.5% w/w, foreign matter 0.1% w/w, dead whole insects 2/100 g, visible mould damage 1.0% w/w, mammalian excreta 2.2 mg/kg, insect damaged leaves 1.0% w/w, other excreta 4.4 mg/kg and live insects 0; Crushed/Rubbed/Flaked and Ground/Powdered forms have specified limits or marked N/A where not evaluated.
Annex I — heading only; no substantive content provided in this section.
